document: Phase 0 — Python From Zero to Chatbot-Ready
goal: Every member writes Python confidently and is ready to build Friday

setup.title: Before Week 1 — Setup Guide
steps: Install Python 3.11 from python.org. During installation check Add Python to PATH. Verify by typing python --version in terminal.
Install VS Code from code.visualstudio.com. Install extensions: Python by Microsoft, Pylance, Python Indent, GitLens.
Install Git from git-scm.com. Run: git config --global user.name and git config --global user.email in terminal.
Create a free GitHub account at github.com. Share your username in the team group.
Shuv adds all members to the friday-ai GitHub repository as collaborators. Everyone runs git clone.
Inside the repo create a folder called practice/your_name/ for all daily practice code.
Open VS Code, create hello.py, type print('Hello from your name') and press F5 to run. If you see your message setup is complete.

c_vs_python.title: C vs Python — What Your Team Already Knows

comparison.c: int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; }
comparison.python: def add(a, b): return a + b

weeks.title: Python Basics — Variables, Data Types and Input/Output
weeks.goal: Every member writes a program that asks for name and age then prints a personalised message.

concepts: What is a variable — a box that stores a value
Four basic data types: int, float, str, bool
type() function — tells you what kind of data something is
C comparison: in C you wrote int x = 5 — in Python just write x = 5
days.practice: Create 5 variables: your name, age, height, is_student, gpa — print each one

concepts: String basics — text in single or double quotes
String concatenation — joining strings with +
f-strings — inserting variable values directly into text
String methods: .upper(), .lower(), .strip(), .replace(), .split()
String length with len()
days.practice: Ask user for full name. Print it in uppercase, lowercase, and count its length.

days.topic: Input from User and Type Conversion
concepts: input() function — pauses program and waits for user to type
input always returns a string even if user types a number
Type conversion: int(), float(), str()
Common mistake: cannot do math with input directly — convert first
days.practice: Ask user for two numbers. Print their sum, difference, product, and division.

concepts: if statement — runs code only when condition is True
elif and else — check more conditions or handle no match
Comparison operators: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=
Logical operators: and, or, not
C comparison: if (x > 5) {} becomes if x > 5: with indentation
days.practice: Write a grade calculator — ask for marks, print A/B/C/D/F based on score.

requirements: Ask for name, age, and city
Greet the user by name
Tell them if they are a minor or adult based on age
Ask a simple math question and check the answer
Ask them to rate their day from 1 to 10
Respond differently based on their rating
Push completed project to GitHub by Sunday night

weeks.title: Loops, Lists and Functions
weeks.goal: Every member writes a contact book program using lists and functions.

concepts: for loop with range() — runs a fixed number of times
for loop with a list — loops through each item
while loop — keeps running while condition is True
break — exits loop immediately
continue — skips to next iteration
C comparison: identical logic, only syntax changes
days.practice: Print multiplication table for any number the user enters.

days.topic: Lists — Python's Most Used Data Structure
concepts: Creating a list — ordered collection of any data type
Accessing items by index — starts at 0 like C arrays
append(), remove(), pop() — adding and removing items
len() — number of items in list
Looping through a list
List slicing — getting a portion of the list
days.practice: Create list of 5 team member names. Print numbered. Add one. Remove one. Print again.

concepts: Stores data as key:value pairs like a contact book
Creating, accessing, adding, and updating dictionary entries
Looping through keys and values with .items()
Safe access with .get() to avoid errors
Why critical for AI — all API responses come back as dictionaries
days.practice: Create a dictionary for each team member with name, role, and favourite language.

concepts: Why functions — write once, use many times
def keyword — defining a function
Parameters and arguments
return statement — sending values back
Default parameters — fallback values if nothing passed
C comparison: same concept but no need to declare return type
days.practice: Write 5 functions: add, subtract, multiply, divide, power. Test each with user input.

requirements: Menu: Add Contact, View All, Search, Delete, Exit
Each contact stores name, phone number, email
add_contact() function
view_contacts() function
search_contact(name) function
delete_contact(name) function
Menu loops until user chooses Exit
Push to GitHub by Sunday night

weeks.title: Object Oriented Programming and File Handling
weeks.goal: Every member builds a Student Management System using classes.

concepts: Class — a blueprint for creating objects
Object — a specific instance of a class
__init__ method — constructor, runs when object is created
self keyword — refers to the current object
C comparison: similar to structs but more powerful
days.practice: Create a Person class with name, age, city. Create 3 Person objects. Print details.

days.topic: Class Methods, Class Variables and Inheritance
concepts: Instance variables vs class variables
Multiple methods in a class
Inheritance — child class gets all parent properties
super() — calls the parent class method
Why important for Path 1 — LangChain and FastAPI use classes everywhere
days.practice: Create Animal class. Create Dog and Cat classes that inherit from Animal.

concepts: Why files matter — chatbot saves memory to files
open() function with modes: r, w, a
with statement — automatically closes file when done
f.read() and f.readlines()
f.write() for writing text to files
days.practice: Save names to a file then read them back and print numbered.

days.topic: Working with JSON — The Language of APIs
concepts: JSON is the standard format for sending data between programs and APIs
Looks like a Python dictionary
json.loads() — converts JSON string to Python dictionary
json.dumps() — converts Python dictionary to JSON string
json.dump() and json.load() for saving and reading JSON files
Critical for Path 1 — every Gemini API response is JSON
days.practice: Create dictionary of 3 team members. Save to JSON file. Read back and print.

requirements: Student class with name, roll_number, marks list
average() method returns average marks
grade() method returns A/B/C/D/F
to_dict() method converts object to dictionary
display() method prints all details
Add new students
Save all students to JSON file
Load students from JSON on startup
Find student with highest average
Push to GitHub by Sunday night

weeks.title: Error Handling, Modules and pip Packages
weeks.goal: Write code that does not crash. Use other people's libraries.
weeks.resource: Harry's Python course — Error Handling and Modules sections

concepts: Why errors happen — bad input, missing files, network failures
try / except — risky code in try, fallback in except
Specific exceptions: ValueError, FileNotFoundError
finally block — runs whether or not an error occurred
raise statement — create and throw your own errors
days.practice: Rewrite Week 1 calculator with full error handling — division by zero and non-number input.

days.topic: Modules — Organising Your Code into Files
concepts: Any Python file is a module
import statement — makes another file's code available
from import — imports only what you need
Standard library modules: os, sys, datetime, math, random
__name__ == '__main__' — runs only when file is executed directly
days.practice: Split Week 3 Student project into 3 files: student.py, database.py, main.py

concepts: pip is Python's package manager — like an app store for libraries
pip install — installs any package from the internet
requirements.txt — lists all packages the project needs
Virtual environments — isolated package space per project
Activating venv on Windows and Mac/Linux
days.practice: Create venv for Friday project. Install requests, python-dotenv, rich, httpx. Create requirements.txt.

concepts: Environment variables store secrets outside your code
Never put API keys directly in code — anyone can steal them from GitHub
.env file stores all secret values locally
python-dotenv loads .env into your program
os.getenv() retrieves environment variable values safely
Always add .env to .gitignore
days.practice: Create .env with 3 fake API keys. Load and print them. Add .env to .gitignore.

requirements: Use requests library to call Open-Meteo free weather API
Ask user for city name
Get current temperature, wind speed, weather condition
Print a nicely formatted weather report
Save result to weather_log.json file
Full error handling: no internet, city not found, API down
Push to GitHub by Sunday night

weeks.title: APIs, Requests and Working with Real Data
weeks.goal: Make real API calls and handle real-world data confidently.

days.key_concepts: GET vs POST, status codes, headers
requests.get(), requests.post(), response.json(), parameters
days.project: Build a currency converter using API

days.key_concepts: JSON body, authentication headers, nested JSON responses
days.project: Send data to httpbin.org test API

days.key_concepts: async/await, asyncio, aiohttp for async requests
Full async news client

weeks.title: Gemini API Deep Dive
weeks.goal: Build Friday's first working terminal chatbot using Gemini.

days.key_concepts: API key from aistudio.google.com, google-generativeai library
Passing previous messages, role types: user and model
Giving Gemini a personality, temperature and safety settings
Getting words as they generate, real-time display
Full terminal chatbot with personality, memory, streaming, error handling

weeks.title: FastAPI and Connecting Frontend to Backend
weeks.goal: Build a production-ready backend API for Friday.

days.key_concepts: Endpoints, GET vs POST, path parameters, automatic docs at /docs
Request and response models, validation, type hints
Moving chatbot logic into endpoint, CORS middleware
Real-time two-way communication, why better than polling for chat
Complete backend with /chat, /clear, /status, error handling, logging

weeks.title: Final Integration and Path 1 Readiness Test
weeks.goal: Every member independently builds a simple Gemini chatbot API with memory in 3 hours.

days.key_concepts: Branching, pull requests, merge conflicts, team GitHub workflow
days.project: Team practices merging all Week 7 work

days.key_concepts: Clean code, readable functions, naming conventions, comments, docstrings
days.project: Refactor Week 6 project with clean code

days.key_concepts: VS Code debugger, print debugging, reading error tracebacks
Each member identifies weakest area and spends full day practicing
All 6 members independently build Gemini chatbot API with memory in 3 hours

readiness_test_requirements: Create a Python virtual environment and install required packages
Set up a .env file and load API keys securely
Make a POST request to the Gemini API and print the response
Build a multi-turn conversation with memory using a list of messages
Create a FastAPI server with a /chat endpoint
Handle errors gracefully — no crashes on bad input
Push all code to GitHub with a clear README

blocks.activity: Review yesterday — read through old code without running it. Can you explain every line?
Learn today's topic — type every code example yourself, never copy-paste. Run it, break it, fix it.
Practice exercise — complete the day's exercise. Commit and push to GitHub every single day.

team_rules: Code every single day — even 30 minutes
Push at least one commit to GitHub every working day
Sunday 30-minute team video call — each member demos their week project
If stuck for more than 45 minutes, ask a teammate immediately
Shuv teaches the team every Saturday — 45 minutes live coding session
No shame in being stuck — silence wastes everyone's time

resources.name: CS50P Harvard
resources.covers: Structured Python course with problem sets

resources.name: Stack Overflow
resources.covers: When you are stuck — search your error message
